Glossary About
Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Émile Durkheim

1858 – 1917

Collective representations, social consciousness, and the mind shaped by the sacred.

Here is the strongest reconstruction: Durkheim’s scattered remarks on soul, mind, consciousness, religion, categories, morality, suicide, and social facts amount to a social-naturalist philosophy of mind. It is not a Cartesian theory in which an immaterial private soul confronts a material body. It is also not a reductive materialism in which consciousness is merely brain-process or individual psychology. Durkheim’s central claim is that distinctively human consciousness is duplex: one pole is organic, sensory, appetitive, and individual; the other is conceptual, moral, symbolic, sacred, and social. What religion calls the “soul” is, on his account, the symbolic recognition that the individual is inhabited by forces, ideals, concepts, and obligations that exceed the organism and yet become interior to it. In his strongest formulation, the soul is “a portion of the collective soul” individualized in each person, while conceptual and moral life are the form society takes when it lives inside individual consciousness.

This makes Durkheim’s theology indispensable. His discussions of God, soul, sacredness, ritual, immortality, and “the divinity in us” are not decorative metaphors around an otherwise secular sociology. They are the privileged site where he studies the fact that human beings experience certain representations as superior, obligatory, impersonal, energizing, and set apart. Durkheim’s controversial move is to say that theology is neither simply false nor literally true in its own terms. Religion mistakes the symbol for the source, but the source is real: the sacred is the form under which society, collective life, and collective ideals become sensible to individuals.

1. The terminological problem: soul, mind, conscience, consciousness

Durkheim writes in French, and several key terms resist clean translation. Âme means soul. Esprit can mean mind, spirit, or intellect depending on context. Conscience means both consciousness and moral conscience. Conscience collective is therefore not merely “collective consciousness” in a cognitive sense; it is also collective conscience, the shared moral-emotional life of a group. This ambiguity is philosophically important. Durkheim does not sharply separate awareness, cognition, normativity, and moral pressure. For him, the mind is not only a representational apparatus; it is also a bearer of obligations, ideals, affects, prohibitions, and sacred distinctions.

The technical term that often does the deepest work is représentation. Individual and collective life are both made of representations, but collective representations are not reducible to private mental images. Durkheim explicitly argues that social life rests on associated individuals as psychic life rests on a physiological substrate, yet in both cases the emergent whole has properties not reducible to the substrate. Collective representations arise from interactions among individual consciousnesses, but they “overflow” each particular consciousness.

This means that a Durkheimian philosophy of mind must not begin with an isolated subject asking how it knows an external world. It begins with a social being whose capacities for reason, duty, selfhood, and even interiority have been formed by language, rites, education, law, symbols, and collective emotions. In The Rules of Sociological Method, Durkheim defines social facts as ways of acting, thinking, and feeling that exist outside individual consciousnesses and exercise coercive power over them; he gives religious beliefs, moral duties, legal rules, monetary conventions, and professional practices as examples.

The crucial point is that “outside” does not mean merely external in space. Social facts are first encountered as outside the individual, but they become internalized through education, habit, ritual, and participation. Durkheim says that children are trained into ways of seeing, feeling, and acting that they would not have reached spontaneously; education forms the social being within the individual.

So Durkheim’s “mind” is not a private container. It is a site of layered formations: organic sensation, individual biography, inherited symbols, social norms, collective categories, sacred ideals, and historically formed representations.

2. Durkheim’s anti-reductive ontology of mind

Durkheim’s philosophy of mind is best described as emergentist. At the individual level, he rejects the reduction of mental life to physiology. At the collective level, he rejects the reduction of social life to individual psychology. The same logical pattern governs both arguments.

In “Individual and Collective Representations,” Durkheim argues that representations depend on neural and bodily conditions but have a relative autonomy. He explicitly says that there is no need to imagine a soul separated from the body, yet he also insists that thoughts cannot be localized as if each idea were simply lodged in a particular cellular point. Mental life is “in” the brain, but not in a simple mechanical way.

This is not spiritualism in the traditional metaphysical sense. Durkheim does not posit an immaterial soul-substance floating free from the body. But it is also not eliminative materialism. He wants to preserve what older metaphysics called the “spirituality” of mind by translating it into a positive scientific claim: representations have their own mode of association, persistence, force, and transformation. They are conditioned by physiology without being exhausted by physiology.

He then applies the same reasoning to society. Collective representations arise from the association of individual consciousnesses, but the synthesis produces something new. Private sentiments become social by entering a field of interaction; once socialized, they return to individuals with a power and authority no individual alone could generate. Durkheim compares this to the emergence of mental life from the association of neural elements. The collective is not an aggregate of already complete private minds; it is a new level of psychic reality.

This gives us Durkheim’s basic ontological schema:

Body and brain generate the conditions for individual mental life.

Individual representations emerge from physiological life and acquire relative autonomy.

Collective representations emerge from the association of individual consciousnesses and acquire relative autonomy.

The individual human mind, once socialized, becomes the place where organic, individual, and collective representations intersect.

Durkheim explicitly rejects both individualist reduction and theological holism. He criticizes any view that would explain the complex by reducing it to the simple, whether that means reducing society to individuals or deriving individuals from an abstract whole. Social facts must be explained by society, mental facts by the specific synthesis that produces mental life, and vital facts by the combinations proper to living systems.

The resulting position is a layered naturalism. Durkheim is a naturalist because he refuses supernatural explanation. But he is not a flat naturalist. For him, nature contains emergent levels: life, mind, and society. Each has its own regularities.

3. Collective consciousness: not a metaphor, but not a group brain

The phrase collective consciousness is often misunderstood. Durkheim does not mean that society has a literal brain hovering above individual persons. He means that a society has a relatively organized system of beliefs, sentiments, classifications, ideals, and values that is realized in individuals but not reducible to any one of them.

In The Division of Labour in Society, he defines the common or collective consciousness as the ensemble of beliefs and sentiments common to the average members of a society. It forms a determinate system with “a life of its own”; it is diffuse across the whole society, has specific characteristics, and is realized in individuals without belonging exclusively to any one individual.

This collective consciousness is most intense in societies governed by what Durkheim calls mechanical solidarity. There, the individual consciousness depends heavily on the collective type, and the person is almost absorbed by the shared moral-religious order.

In societies governed by organic solidarity, however, the division of labor differentiates persons and functions. The common consciousness becomes less concrete and less totalizing. It leaves more room for individuality, specialization, dissent, and personal development. Durkheim is therefore not simply a theorist of social conformity. He thinks individuality itself grows historically through social differentiation. The more specialized activity becomes, the more personal consciousness can develop.

This point matters for philosophy of mind. Durkheim does not hold that society crushes mind from outside. Rather, society produces the conditions under which certain kinds of mind become possible. In archaic or highly integrated societies, consciousness is more saturated by shared sacred representations. In modern societies, consciousness becomes more individualized, reflective, and abstract. But even modern individuality remains socially produced; it is not a pre-social atom.

French secondary literature has rightly emphasized this tension. One French interpretation argues that Durkheim should not be read as a simple thinker of homogeneity, because his sociology is structured by a tension between individual and collective personality. The same discussion warns against taking the “collective soul” as a literal supra-individual person.

That is exactly the right caution. Durkheim’s collective consciousness is real, but its reality is emergent, distributed, symbolic, affective, and normative. It is not a second organism. It is a structured field of representations that lives through individuals while exceeding each of them.

4. The soul as the individualization of collective force

Durkheim’s most direct philosophy of the soul appears in The Elementary Forms of Religious Life and in his 1907 lectures on the origins of religious life. His theory begins from the totemic case: clans identify themselves with a totemic emblem, animal, plant, or symbol. Durkheim argues that the totem symbolizes both the clan and the sacred principle or “god” of the clan. The famous thesis is that the god and the society are, at this level, the same reality apprehended under different signs.

The soul is then derived from this sacred collective principle. Durkheim’s argument is not that primitive people invented the soul because they saw dreams, shadows, or reflections and confused them with a double. He rejects the idea that dream-experience alone could generate such a durable religious belief. The deeper problem is why a person is felt to contain a sacred principle distinct from the profane body.

His answer is that the soul is the totemic principle individualized. In Elementary Forms, he says that the soul is the totemic principle incarnated in each individual. The individual contains an anonymous sacred force, the same force that animates the clan, ancestors, sacred objects, and the totem itself.

The 1907 French course states the same thesis with particular clarity: there is not a new soul created for every birth; rather, there is a stock of ancestral souls, and the newborn is a kind of avatar. Durkheim says there that the soul is the totemic divinity incarnated in each individual and individualized in him or her. The totemic principle, which is ultimately the clan, can live only in individuals; it fragments itself, and each fragment is a soul.

This is the key to Durkheim’s entire philosophy of soul. The soul is not a private metaphysical substance. It is a social-sacred force individualized in the person.

That also explains the soul’s strange dual phenomenology. It is experienced as the most intimate thing in us, yet also as something superior to us, not fully ours, and capable of commanding us. Durkheim connects this with moral conscience: when conscience speaks, it speaks with a voice that is ours and not ours. It is within us, but it has an authority that surpasses our private desires. He calls this “the divinity in us” and identifies the individual soul as a portion of the collective soul.

Durkheim’s account also explains why the soul is sacred while the body is profane. In the religious imagination, the body belongs to ordinary material life, appetite, need, and individuality. The soul belongs to the sacred world because it is made of the same substance as sacred beings. In his late essay on human dualism, Durkheim says that the universal belief in a body-soul duality cannot be treated as a mere illusion; it expresses a real duality in human nature.

Yet the reality expressed is not the reality posited by traditional metaphysics. The true duality is not between matter and immaterial substance. It is between the organism and society, appetite and morality, sensation and concept, individual impulse and impersonal ideal. The soul is the religious name for the social pole of the person.

5. Homo duplex: Durkheim’s non-Cartesian dualism

Durkheim’s most explicit philosophical statement is “The Dualism of Human Nature and Its Social Conditions.” There he argues that sociology must enter the individual, because society penetrates individual consciousness and shapes many of its essential states. Civilization, he says, is what made the human being human.

The human being is double. On one side are sensations, appetites, bodily impulses, and egoistic tendencies. On the other side are conceptual thought, moral activity, duty, reason, and impersonal ideals. The first side is rooted in the organism. The second side is rooted in society.

This is not Cartesian dualism. The body is not opposed to an immaterial thinking substance. Rather, the individual organism is opposed to the socially formed person. The sacred/profane distinction supplies the religious model: the body is profane, the soul sacred; in secular modernity, Duty and Reason remain sacred even after theological language weakens.

Durkheim’s dualism is therefore:

not matter versus immaterial soul;

not brain versus ghost;

not nature versus supernatural grace;

but organism versus society as two sources of psychic life.

This also explains why human beings experience conflict within themselves. We desire what our bodies want, but we also feel bound by ideals that demand sacrifice. Society asks us to surpass ourselves. The moral life requires discipline, attention, restraint, and effort. Durkheim even treats voluntary attention and sustained effort as historically intensified capacities linked to the pressure and discipline of social life.

This point is easily missed. Durkheim is not merely saying that society represses desire. He is saying that society creates a second psychic register. The human being becomes capable of reason, moral self-command, and ideal aspiration because social representations have entered the individual and reorganized consciousness.

6. Concepts, categories, and the social origin of reason

Durkheim’s philosophy of mind is also a theory of cognition. He is not content to say that society shapes moral life. He argues that society shapes the very categories through which thought becomes rational.

In Elementary Forms, Durkheim argues that conceptual thought connects the variable to the permanent and the individual to the social. Concepts are communicable, stable, and impersonal; they differ from fleeting sensations because they can be shared. He explicitly links major categories such as class, time, space, force, and totality to social life.

The concept of totality, for example, is not derived from a private sensory impression. Durkheim argues that it is abstracted from society, which presents itself as a whole that includes and exceeds individuals. Social life also gives models for classification, spatial ordering, temporal rhythms, and causal force.

He calls collective consciousness “the highest form of psychic life” and even a “consciousness of consciousnesses.” This is not because society literally introspects like a person, but because collective life produces representational forms that individual consciousness alone could not generate. Rational thought is thus historical and social without being arbitrary.

This is one of Durkheim’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind. He offers a social externalism about cognition before that term existed. The contents and structures of thought are not simply inside the individual. They are inherited from a public symbolic order. Language, classification, concepts, and categories are collective achievements that become interior capacities.

At the same time, Durkheim does not collapse truth into individual usefulness. In his lectures on pragmatism and sociology, he sees a parallel between pragmatism and sociology because both treat truth and reason as human and historical. But he insists that the individual is too limited to generate truth and reason alone; their authority must be explained through collective history, not private preference.

So Durkheim’s theory is neither rationalist apriorism nor empiricist sensationalism. Categories are not eternal forms implanted in an isolated mind, but neither are they mere aggregates of sensations. They are social-historical forms that acquire objectivity because they are collective, durable, and institutionally enforced.

7. Religion, God, and the theological grammar of mind

Durkheim’s theology is best read as a theory of how the social becomes inwardly experienced as transcendence. A society can generate feelings of dependence, elevation, power, obligation, and enthusiasm. In religious life, these feelings are projected onto symbols: gods, totems, ancestors, spirits, sacred objects, and souls.

Durkheim argues that the totemic symbol represents both the god and the clan. The religious force is the collective, anonymous force of the group made visible through an emblem. It appears transcendent because it surpasses individuals; it appears immanent because it lives within them.

This is the theological core of Durkheim’s philosophy of mind. The individual experiences sacred force as both outside and inside, superior and intimate. That is exactly the structure of the soul. The soul is “mine,” but it is not merely me. It is the presence in me of a larger moral and symbolic order.

Durkheim is often described as reducing religion to society, but that formulation is too crude. He does not say religion is simply a mistake. He repeatedly insists that a theory making religion a pure illusion commits a fundamental error.

The mistake of religion lies in misidentifying the source of sacred force. Its truth lies in registering the existence of a real force that transforms consciousness. In collective gatherings, people are lifted beyond ordinary life; they feel stronger, more unified, more capable, and closer to an ideal world. Durkheim calls this collective effervescence. The religious idea is born in such moments because collective life changes the intensity and quality of psychic activity.

This is why ritual matters for mind. Ritual is not a secondary expression of belief. It is one of the mechanisms by which belief, affect, sacredness, and personal transformation are produced. Religion’s real function is not primarily to give theoretical knowledge of the world, but to act, strengthen, console, reanimate, and raise the believer above ordinary selfhood.

French reception has emphasized this non-reductive side. Hans Joas, in a French discussion of Durkheim and collective ecstasy, characterizes Durkheim’s account as areligious but not crudely reductive: collective ecstasy generates ideal formations that cannot be reduced to individual or material causes.

This is the interpretive key. Durkheim secularizes theology, but he does not trivialize it. Theological ideas preserve the phenomenology of human self-transcendence. God names society as experienced from within its sacred power. The soul names society individualized in the person. The sacred names collective ideals felt as superior to ordinary life.

8. Sacred/profane as a theory of mental stratification

Durkheim’s sacred/profane distinction is not just a theory of religion. It is also a theory of mental stratification. Human consciousness is divided into regions: some representations are ordinary, utilitarian, private, and everyday; others are set apart, protected, revered, feared, and treated as having authority.

In Elementary Forms, Durkheim defines religion by the division between sacred and profane rather than by belief in spirits or gods. A religion can exist even where spiritual beings are not central; what matters is the system of sacred things and the practices that bind a community around them.

This is decisive for philosophy of mind because it means that sacredness is not primarily an object-property. It is a social-mental status. A thing becomes sacred when collective representations and sentiments concentrate upon it, separate it, protect it, and make it a symbol of the group’s ideal life.

Durkheim later gives the most compact formulation: sacred things are collective ideals fixed onto material objects. Collective ideas and feelings have authority over individual consciousness because they are generated by the communion of consciousnesses; symbols are needed because collective life must become visible and durable.

This yields a theory of symbolic embodiment. Society cannot act on minds without signs, rites, emblems, words, gestures, places, bodies, and objects. The sacred is not a free-floating idea. It is anchored in symbols that focus collective affect.

The same mechanism explains secular sacredness. Duty, reason, human dignity, the nation, law, science, and the person can become sacred when they carry collective ideals. Durkheim’s thought therefore permits a theology of modern secular life: modern societies still sacralize, even when they no longer explain sacredness through traditional gods.

9. The modern cult of the person

Durkheim’s mature account of individuality is one of the most important places where theology and philosophy of mind converge. Modernity does not simply destroy religion; it relocates sacredness onto the person. In The Division of Labour, he argues that as the common consciousness becomes more abstract and less concrete, the individual becomes the object of a kind of religion or cult.

This cult is paradoxical. It is individualistic in its object, because it venerates the dignity, autonomy, and rights of the person. But it is collective in its source, because its authority comes from society. Durkheim explicitly says that the cult of the individual draws its force from society even though it attaches us to ourselves.

In Suicide, he makes the same point in a sharper moral-theological register: the modern person is sacred, and suicide is condemned in part because it violates the cult of the human person. Durkheim connects this with the older idea of an immortal soul understood as a divinity within us.

This means Durkheim does not oppose social formation to individuality. He thinks individuality is itself a social achievement. Modern persons become capable of selfhood because society has generalized and abstracted its sacred ideals. As societies expand, moral and religious ideas become less local and more universal; the notion of “man” replaces narrower civic, tribal, or national types.

A French discussion of Durkheim and Guyau captures this well: society creates individualism, and the person becomes a common denominator with religious character. The individualist sacred is not an escape from society; it is a modern social institution.

For philosophy of mind, this means the “self” is not a primitive datum. The person is the organism plus the socially instituted dignity, rights, responsibilities, ideals, and categories through which the organism comes to understand itself as a self. A Durkheimian self is never merely psychological. It is moral, juridical, symbolic, and sacred.

10. Immortality, ancestors, and the continuity of collective life

Durkheim’s interpretation of immortality is especially important because it shows how far he is from a simple theory of personal survival. He argues that early beliefs in the soul’s persistence after death are not primarily motivated by moral retribution, nor by a strong desire for individual personal immortality. In some cases, the afterlife does not preserve personal identity in the later theological sense.

In the totemic schema, souls often come from ancestors and return to the ancestral stock. Birth is a reincarnation or reindividualization of a sacred principle rather than the creation of a wholly new private soul. Ancestors are quasi-divine because they are earlier embodiments of the same collective sacred force.

This has a strong philosophical implication. The soul’s immortality expresses the continuity of the group more than the persistence of a private ego. The individual dies, but the sacred principle continues to circulate through new individuals, symbols, rites, and generations. The religious idea of soul-survival is therefore a symbolic form of social continuity.

Spanish secondary discussion makes this point clearly: in Durkheim, the survival of the soul is less a matter of reward or punishment than of group continuity, and the individual soul is superior to the body because it comes from ancestral, tutelary, or totemic sources.

This does not mean Durkheim denies the emotional power of death, mourning, or personal memory. Rather, he interprets them within a larger symbolic economy: the dead remain socially real because collective representations preserve them and because the group’s sacred life is imagined as passing through them.

11. Emotion, effervescence, and the energetic mind

Durkheim’s philosophy of mind is not intellectualist. It is deeply affective. He repeatedly emphasizes that collective life changes the intensity of psychic life. Ritual gatherings do not simply communicate ideas; they generate energy.

In moments of collective effervescence, individuals feel transported beyond ordinary life. The group’s energy amplifies individual emotions, fuses participants, and produces an idealized world. Sacred symbols then retain and stabilize these energies after the gathering disperses.

This gives Durkheim an affective theory of representation. Representations are not cold mental pictures. They carry force. They can attract, repel, command, console, exalt, terrify, and bind. Collective representations are especially powerful because they are charged by shared emotion and institutional repetition.

This is also why Durkheim’s theory of mind is inseparable from ritual. Ritual periodically renews the affective charge of symbols. It reanimates the believer and strengthens attachment to the group. Religion, he says, raises the person above ordinary selfhood and increases vital tone.

The mind, for Durkheim, is therefore not only representational and normative. It is also energetic. Social life produces psychic intensities that individuals experience as inspiration, duty, enthusiasm, reverence, fear, shame, courage, or consolation.

12. Desire, regulation, suicide, and the pathology of consciousness

Durkheim’s theory of mind also includes a social theory of psychic suffering. Suicide is not only a sociological study of death rates; it is also a theory of how consciousness can become disordered when social integration and regulation fail.

Durkheim argues that the suicide rate, though composed of individual acts, is a social fact with its own regularity. It expresses a state of the collective soul or collective moral life.

His most philosophically important claim concerns desire. Human appetites, unlike purely organic needs, are not naturally self-limiting. If social regulation weakens, desire becomes indeterminate. Durkheim famously describes unregulated sensibility as a kind of bottomless abyss. Economic crises, rapid change, and chronic deregulation can therefore intensify suffering not simply by causing material deprivation but by disorganizing the moral limits that make desire livable.

This is a major contribution to philosophy of mind. For Durkheim, the psyche requires social form. Desire is not self-regulating. The individual needs external moral limits that become internalized as expectations, disciplines, and ideals. When those limits collapse, consciousness becomes unstable.

The currents that produce suicide are not metaphors, Durkheim insists. They are real moral and social forces that dominate particular consciousnesses. Individual acts are prolongations of social states; currents of egoism, altruism, and anomie penetrate individuals.

Spanish reception of Durkheim’s suicide theory has emphasized this dualist psychology: bodily instincts and mental faculties require social discipline, and where passions are not socially regulated, they become insatiable.

A Durkheimian philosophy of mind therefore includes a theory of mental pathology as social dysregulation. Suffering is not merely inside the individual; it can be generated by the weakening, contradiction, or violence of collective norms.

13. A systematic reconstruction: Durkheimian social emergentism

Durkheim never wrote a book called Philosophy of Mind. But a comprehensive philosophy can be reconstructed from his work. Its core theses are these.

First, mind is representational but not merely individual. Mental life consists in representations, but representations can be individual or collective. Collective representations are not reducible to private mental contents because they arise from social synthesis and return to individuals as public, durable, authoritative forms.

Second, mental life is emergent. Individual consciousness depends on the body and brain, but is not reducible to cellular mechanics. Collective consciousness depends on individual consciousnesses, but is not reducible to them. Durkheim uses the same anti-reductive logic at both levels.

Third, the human person is duplex. One pole is organic, sensory, appetitive, and egoistic. The other is social, moral, conceptual, and impersonal. This duality is the empirical basis behind religious body-soul dualism.

Fourth, the soul is the social sacred individualized. In religious language, the soul is a sacred principle inside the person. In sociological language, it is the internalization and individualization of collective force, collective ideals, and collective representations.

Fifth, God is society symbolized, but not society trivialized. The god of the clan is the clan itself apprehended through a sacred emblem; more generally, divinity names the power of collective life to elevate, constrain, and transform consciousness. Religion is false if taken literally as theology of supernatural beings, but true as symbolic apprehension of social-moral reality.

Sixth, reason is social-historical. Categories, concepts, language, classification, and totality are collective achievements. Rationality is not merely private cognition; it is the interiorization of public symbolic forms.

Seventh, normativity is experienced as sacred authority. Duties, moral rules, and ideals command the individual because they come from a collective source that exceeds private desire. Even secular duty and reason retain the structure of sacredness.

Eighth, symbols are necessary vehicles of mind. Collective life cannot act directly on consciousness without emblems, rites, names, gestures, institutions, and material supports. The symbol concentrates affect and makes the group visible to itself.

Ninth, affect is constitutive, not accidental. Collective effervescence generates ideals, intensifies representation, and gives sacred symbols their force. Mind is not merely cognitive structure; it is charged by social emotion.

Tenth, individuality is socially produced. Modern individuality is not the pre-social foundation of society. It is a historical product of social differentiation and of the modern cult of the person. The individual becomes sacred because society has made personhood into a collective ideal.

Eleventh, mental pathology can be social in origin. Egoism, anomie, and excessive altruism are not merely private disorders. They are social currents that penetrate consciousness and shape vulnerability, desire, despair, and self-destruction.

Twelfth, Durkheim’s theory is a naturalized theology of the soul. It preserves the phenomena theology describes—transcendence, sacredness, inward divinity, moral command, ritual transformation, immortality, communion—but translates their source into collective life.

14. What Durkheim rejects

Durkheim’s position becomes clearer if we identify the alternatives he rejects.

He rejects Cartesian substance dualism. The soul is not an immaterial thing separable from the body in the metaphysical sense. Durkheim explicitly says that we need not imagine a soul apart from the body in order to explain the relative autonomy of thought.

He rejects psychological individualism. Social life is not built out of isolated individual minds whose contents are already complete. The association of consciousnesses creates new representations, meanings, and forces.

He rejects reductive materialism. Mental and social realities depend on lower-level substrates but have their own causal properties. Representations are real forces.

He rejects religion-as-pure-illusion. Religion misrecognizes its object, but the experience of sacred force is grounded in real collective forces.

He rejects asocial rationalism. Reason is not an eternal private faculty detached from history. The categories of thought are formed in collective life.

He rejects asocial individualism. The modern individual is not society’s opposite. The sacred person is one of society’s own creations.

15. Theological seriousness: Durkheim as neither atheist debunker nor orthodox theologian

Durkheim’s theological remarks demand careful treatment because his position is unstable in a productive way. From the standpoint of orthodox theology, he appears reductive: God, soul, and sacred beings are interpreted as symbolic forms of society. From the standpoint of secular reductionism, however, he preserves too much of religion: sacredness is real, moral authority is real, ritual transformation is real, and the individual really is inhabited by something greater than private appetite.

The best description is that Durkheim gives a sociological transposition of theology. He moves theological predicates onto society:

God becomes collective life as experienced in its authority, beneficence, and power.

The soul becomes the collective sacred individualized in the person.

The sacred becomes the status of collective ideals set apart from ordinary utility.

Ritual becomes the technique by which collective force is regenerated and internalized.

Immortality becomes the continuity of collective life through generations, ancestors, and symbols.

Grace, in a secularized sense, becomes the surplus of energy that collective life gives to the individual.

This is why Durkheim can say religion is not primarily a system of speculative ideas but a system of forces that make people act and live differently. Religion strengthens the believer because the believer is in relation with a real source of energy: the group, its ideals, and its symbols.

The theological question then becomes: is society merely replacing God, or is Durkheim discovering the social medium through which divine experience is historically mediated? Durkheim himself chooses the first answer in sociological terms. But his own analysis leaves room for a more theological reading: even if one believes in a transcendent God, Durkheim can still be read as explaining why divine authority is experienced through collective symbols, rites, moral communities, and sacred forms.

That is why theological comments should not be treated as distractions. They are the very grammar of Durkheim’s account of mind. Without soul, sacredness, god, ritual, and collective effervescence, one loses the phenomenology of authority, elevation, and inward transcendence that his sociology is trying to explain.

16. Non-English reception and interpretive pressure points

The French reception is particularly useful because it often resists the overly simple Anglo-American picture of Durkheim as a theorist of constraint and conformity. The French article on individual and collective personality stresses that Durkheim’s problem is the tension between individual and collective personality, not the erasure of individuality. It also warns that the “collective soul” should not be taken as a literal supra-individual person.

French discussion of Durkheim and Guyau is also helpful because it shows how Durkheim’s theory of individuality is simultaneously religious and sociological. Modern society creates individualism; the person becomes sacred; the soul can be read as an emissary of society within the individual.

Spanish reception adds a useful critical angle. A Spanish discussion of Schutz and Durkheim notes that Schutz saw in Durkheim a powerful account of the moral and social determination of individual consciousness, but also treated the “collective soul” as a possible fiction if reified. This is a fair pressure point: Durkheim’s theory is strongest when collective soul is understood as emergent symbolic reality, weakest when it sounds like a literal group-person.

Spanish discussions of Durkheim’s body-soul dualism and suicide also highlight two important themes: first, the soul’s continuity is connected to the continuity of the group rather than only personal immortality; second, anomie shows that individual consciousness requires social limits in order to avoid pathological expansion of desire.

There are also ethnographic and theoretical limits. Durkheim’s Australian totemic material cannot simply be treated as current anthropology. Some later ethnographic work complicates his sharp emphasis on solidarity by showing ritual relations with spirits that do not fit neatly into his account of collective integration.

Still, the philosophical structure does not stand or fall entirely with every ethnographic detail. The durable contribution is the conceptual architecture: mind as socially formed representation; soul as sacred social interiority; reason as collective category; personhood as socially sacralized individuality; and pathology as failure of integration or regulation.

17. Final synthesis

Durkheim’s comprehensive philosophy of mind can be named sacred social emergentism.

It is social because distinctively human consciousness is formed by collective life: language, categories, norms, symbols, rites, institutions, education, law, and shared ideals.

It is emergentist because mind is not reducible to brain, and collective mind is not reducible to individual psychology. Each level depends on a substrate but generates new properties.

It is sacred because the highest functions of mind—duty, reason, personhood, ideality, reverence, self-transcendence—are experienced through the sacred/profane distinction, whether or not theology remains explicit.

It is naturalist because Durkheim does not invoke supernatural causation.

It is anti-reductive because he refuses to dissolve soul, religion, morality, and reason into illusion.

The soul, in this reconstruction, is the religious symbol of the socialized self. It names the fact that the individual organism is inhabited by collective representations that are older, larger, more authoritative, and more durable than private desire. The mind is not simply inside the skull; it is a node where social forces become conscious. Consciousness is not merely awareness; it is also conscience, obligation, classification, reverence, and participation in a shared symbolic world.

Durkheim’s deepest claim is therefore not “society invented the soul” in a debunking sense. It is that human beings came to experience themselves as souls because they really do contain something more than their private organism: the collective, sacred, moral, and conceptual life of society individualized within them.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
